name: Agile & delivery methodologies
description: Scrum, Kanban, XP and the SDLC models (Waterfall, V-model, iterative, DevOps, SAFe) — process reasoning for senior interviews. From the 'agile' and 'módszertanok' prep docs.

topics.name: Scrum
topics.description: Roles, events and artifacts of the Scrum framework; empirical process control.
topics.studyNotes:
Scrum is empirical process control packaged into a framework: fixed-length sprints (timeboxes), defined roles — Product Owner (what and why, backlog ordering), Scrum Master (process health, impediments), Developers (how) — and events: sprint planning, daily scrum, review (inspect the product), retrospective (inspect the process). Artifacts: product backlog, sprint backlog, increment; each with a commitment (goal / sprint goal / definition of done).

The mechanism underneath the ceremonies: short cycles force inspection and adaptation — every sprint produces a usable increment and two feedback loops, one on the product (review) and one on the way of working (retro).

When Scrum fits: planned feature work in a stable team that benefits from a protected sprint scope and a cadence. When it chafes: interrupt-driven work — support, ops, urgent fixes — where a sprint commitment is fiction from day two; that is Kanban territory.

topics.name: Kanban
topics.description: Flow-based method: visualize work, limit WIP, manage flow.
topics.studyNotes:
Kanban manages FLOW instead of timeboxes: visualize the work on a board, LIMIT work in progress, and manage/measure the flow (lead time, cycle time). No prescribed roles or ceremonies — it wraps around what the team already does; change is evolutionary.

WIP limits are the engine, and the interview question is why they help: less parallel work means less context switching, queues become visible instead of hidden in "in progress", finishing beats starting (pull, not push), and blocked items surface immediately because they visibly clog a column. Counterintuitively, limiting WIP RAISES throughput — Little's Law in practice.

Fit: interrupt-driven and continuous-arrival work — support, operations, maintenance — where priorities shift faster than any sprint could hold. Scrum vs Kanban is not a religion question: cadence and commitment vs continuous flow; many teams run a pragmatic mix (Scrumban).

topics.name: SDLC models
topics.description: Waterfall, V-model, iterative/incremental, spiral and when each applies.
topics.studyNotes:
The models, by their bet on requirements: waterfall — sequential phases, everything specified up front; V-model — waterfall with a test level mirroring each specification level; iterative/incremental — build in slices, learn between them; spiral — iterations explicitly driven by risk analysis; agile — short cycles, evolving requirements, continuous feedback.

The honest selection logic: it depends on requirement STABILITY and the COST OF LATE CHANGE. Fixed, well-understood, contract-bound requirements — a regulated medical device, a fixed-bid tender with heavy audit/documentation demands — genuinely favor plan-driven models; the V-model's spec-to-test traceability exists for exactly those audits. Evolving requirements and product discovery favor agile: short feedback loops keep late change cheap instead of catastrophic.

The mature closing point: real teams run hybrids — plan-driven governance at the milestone level with agile delivery inside; or agile development with a formal verification phase where regulation requires it. Naming a credible hybrid beats declaring one model universally right.

topics.name: DevOps
topics.description: Culture and practices uniting dev and ops: CI/CD, automation, shared ownership.
topics.studyNotes:
DevOps is a culture before it is a toolchain: tearing down the wall between "dev throws it over" and "ops runs it". The slogan that captures it — "you build it, you run it": the team that writes the software owns it in production, pager included. Shared ownership changes incentives; tooling merely enables it.

The practices that make it real:
- CI/CD — every change built, tested and deployable automatically; deploys are boring and frequent, not quarterly events.
- Infrastructure as code — environments versioned and reproducible, not hand-crafted.
- Observability and fast feedback — monitoring, alerting, quick rollback; production tells you the truth quickly.

How to measure whether it is working — the DORA four: deployment frequency, lead time for changes, change failure rate, time to restore. Improving those numbers IS improving DevOps; a tool purchase without them moving is theater.

topics.name: Agile Manifesto, User Stories & Estimation
topics.description: The four values and twelve principles; INVEST stories, acceptance criteria, story points, planning poker, velocity.
topics.studyNotes:
The Agile Manifesto (2001, seventeen practitioners — Schwaber, Sutherland, Fowler, Martin among them) is a VALUE SYSTEM, not a methodology. The four values, each "X over Y" where Y still matters: individuals and interactions over processes and tools; working software over comprehensive documentation; customer collaboration over contract negotiation; responding to change over following a plan.

The twelve principles reduce to a handful of habits: deliver early and continuously (a working version every couple of weeks beats a big release after six months); welcome changing requirements — the backlog is a living document; working software is THE measure of progress (not documents or meetings); business and technical people work together daily; sustainable pace (crunch is the enemy); continuous attention to technical excellence — clean code, refactoring, TDD, CI, tech-debt work every sprint; simplicity — YAGNI, maximize the work not done; self-organizing teams (management gives the WHAT and WHY, never the HOW); and regular reflection — the retrospective.

## User stories and estimation

The story format: As a [role], I want [capability], so that [business value]. Good stories pass INVEST: Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small (fits a sprint), Testable. Acceptance criteria make "testable" concrete — Given/When/Then, error cases included. The Definition of Done is the quality bar on top: code reviewed, tests green, CI passed, staging-verified, PO-accepted.

Story points measure RELATIVE size — complexity + effort + uncertainty — never time. Fibonacci scale (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21) with reference anchors: 1 = a button-text fix, 5 = complete registration (API + UI + tests), 13 = a payment flow. Infinity means split it; "?" means run a spike first.

Planning poker exists to prevent anchoring: everyone picks a card IN SECRET, all reveal at once; big spreads (3 vs 13) are discussed by the extremes and re-voted.

Velocity is the team's average completed points over the last 3–5 sprints — a PLANNING tool: it tells the team what to commit to next sprint. The abuses to name: comparing velocities between teams (points are relative, per-team), demanding velocity increases (breeds point inflation), and treating it as a KPI.

topics.name: Extreme Programming & Lean
topics.description: XP's technical practices (TDD, pairing, CI, refactoring) and Lean's waste-elimination principles.
topics.studyNotes:
XP (Kent Beck, 1996) is the agile framework that puts TECHNICAL excellence first — programming practices over management. Five values: communication, simplicity (YAGNI), feedback, courage (refactor boldly, throw code away), respect.

The practices that survive in every modern team, whatever it calls itself:
- Pair programming — driver + navigator on one machine, swapping regularly: quality plus knowledge sharing.
- TDD — the red → green → refactor loop: write a FAILING test, write the minimal code that passes, clean up with tests staying green, repeat. Never code without a test; never more code than the test demands.
- Continuous integration — commit, build and test several times a day; the code is always integrable (its absence is the "big bang integration" anti-pattern).
- Refactoring — continuous improvement without behaviour change, keeping tech debt down.
- Plus: simple design, collective code ownership (no "my code"), coding standards, small frequent releases, on-site customer, sustainable pace.

The practical combo to name: Scrum + XP — Scrum provides the frame (sprints, roles, ceremonies), XP provides the engineering discipline inside it.

## Lean

Adapted from the Toyota Production System (Poppendieck, 2003): everything that does not add customer value is WASTE. Seven principles: eliminate waste, build quality in (TDD, CI, review), create knowledge (pairing, retros), defer commitment (decide when you know the most), deliver fast (small batches), respect people, optimize the WHOLE (the full flow, not just the coding step).

The seven wastes translated to software: unused features (most features are rarely used), partially done work (WIP), waiting (for review, for deploy approval, for environments), extra processes (approval layers, over-documentation), task switching (one developer on three projects), defects (later found = costlier), manual work (hand deploys, hand testing). Kanban's WIP limits and DevOps automation are both direct attacks on this list.

topics.name: Agile Metrics, Scaling & Anti-patterns
topics.description: Burndown/burnup, lead/cycle time, CFD; Scrum of Scrums, LeSS, SAFe PI Planning; the classic failure modes.
topics.studyNotes:
## Metrics

Burndown chart: remaining work through the sprint against an ideal line — above the line means the sprint goal is at risk. Burnup chart: completed work climbing toward a separate total-scope line — its advantage is that SCOPE CHANGES stay visible instead of hiding the progress. Scrum-side metrics: velocity, sprint burndown, sprint-goal success rate. Kanban-side: lead time (backlog → done), cycle time (in-progress → done), throughput (items per unit time), and the cumulative flow diagram — the tool for SPOTTING BOTTLENECKS (a widening band is a clogged column). Both sides: escaped defects. Metrics are diagnostics, not targets.

## Scaling — multiple teams, one product

- Scrum of Scrums: the simplest — each team sends an ambassador to a 15–30 minute coordination standup 2–3× a week: what did the team do, what will it do, impediments, dependencies on other teams.
- LeSS (2–8 teams): minimal rules — ONE product backlog, ONE Product Owner across teams.
- SAFe (50–125+ people): the heavyweight — Agile Release Trains, portfolio level, and PI Planning: a two-day, all-teams, face-to-face event every quarter — business context, vision, team breakouts planning ~5 sprints, dependency mapping on a program board, risks, and a confidence vote (fist of five; average under 3 → re-plan). Pragmatic ranking: start with Scrum of Scrums; adopt SAFe only at genuine organizational scale.

## Anti-patterns — the "what NOT to do" list

Organizational: "Agile in name only" (waterfall with standups); "Scrum-but" (Scrum without retro / PO / review — it is a package, not a menu); micromanagement (management dictating the HOW); velocity pressure (→ point inflation); "no time to refactor" (→ reserve capacity for tech debt every sprint).

Sprint-level: overcommitting beyond velocity; scope creep mid-sprint (new work goes to the NEXT sprint); chronic carry-over (stories too big); skipping the retro (the heart of improvement); 45-minute standups (15 max — details go to a parking lot).

Technical: no CI/CD, no tests, big-bang integration. The refrain: the ceremonies without the values and the engineering are theater.

questions.title: Scrum vs Kanban
questions.prompt: What is the difference between Scrum and Kanban, and how would you choose between them for a team?
questions.answerStructureHint: Cadence/sprints and roles vs continuous flow and WIP limits; which fits planned work vs interrupt-driven work.

expectedPoints.label: Scrum = timeboxed sprints, roles, ceremonies
expectedPoints.description: Fixed-length sprints, defined roles and events, commitment per sprint.

expectedPoints.label: Kanban = continuous flow with WIP limits
expectedPoints.description: Continuous pull, visualize the board, limit work in progress, optimize flow.

expectedPoints.label: Matches method to the work
expectedPoints.description: Scrum for planned feature work in a stable team; Kanban for interrupt-driven/support/ops work.

followUps.prompt: Why do WIP limits actually improve throughput?

questions.title: Agile vs Waterfall
questions.prompt: When would a waterfall or V-model approach be a better fit than agile?
questions.answerStructureHint: Fixed vs evolving requirements, regulatory/contractual constraints, cost of late change.

expectedPoints.label: Depends on requirement stability
expectedPoints.description: Waterfall fits fixed, well-understood requirements; agile fits evolving ones.

expectedPoints.label: Regulatory / contractual / safety context
expectedPoints.description: Heavy documentation, audits, safety-critical or fixed-bid contracts favor plan-driven models.

expectedPoints.label: Cost of late change
expectedPoints.description: Waterfall makes late changes expensive; agile keeps feedback loops short to reduce that risk.

followUps.prompt: In practice teams often mix these. What does a pragmatic hybrid look like?

questions.title: What DevOps actually means
questions.prompt: What does DevOps mean beyond tooling, and what practices make it work?
questions.answerStructureHint: Shared ownership culture, CI/CD automation, fast feedback, observability — not just a job title.

expectedPoints.label: Culture of shared ownership
expectedPoints.description: Breaks the dev/ops wall; teams own their software in production ('you build it, you run it').
acceptedSignals: you build it you run it

expectedPoints.label: CI/CD and automation
expectedPoints.description: Automated pipelines: build, test, deploy; infrastructure as code.

expectedPoints.label: Fast feedback and observability
expectedPoints.description: Monitoring, quick rollbacks and short feedback loops to production.

followUps.prompt: How would you measure whether a team's DevOps is actually improving?

questions.title: Story points and planning poker
questions.prompt: How does story-point estimation work, what makes a good user story, and what should velocity be used for — and not used for?

expectedPoints.label: Points are relative, not time
expectedPoints.description: Complexity + effort + uncertainty on a Fibonacci scale with reference stories.

expectedPoints.label: Poker prevents anchoring
expectedPoints.description: Secret simultaneous votes; extremes explain; re-vote to consensus.

expectedPoints.label: Velocity for planning only
expectedPoints.description: 3–5 sprint average guides commitment; never compare teams or push it as a KPI.

questions.title: TDD's red-green-refactor
questions.prompt: Describe the TDD cycle and the XP practices you would bring into any team — and where Lean's 'waste' idea connects.

expectedPoints.label: Red → green → refactor
expectedPoints.description: Failing test first, minimal pass, cleanup with green tests; no code without a test.

expectedPoints.label: XP practice set
expectedPoints.description: Pairing, CI several times daily, continuous refactoring, collective ownership, small releases.

expectedPoints.label: Lean waste connection
expectedPoints.description: Unused features, WIP, waiting, task switching, manual work — practices exist to eliminate waste.

questions.title: Coordinating multiple agile teams
questions.prompt: Three teams now work on one product. How do you coordinate them, which metrics do you watch, and which anti-patterns do you guard against?

expectedPoints.label: Right-sized scaling mechanism
expectedPoints.description: Scrum of Scrums ambassadors first; LeSS one-backlog-one-PO; SAFe/PI Planning only at real scale.

expectedPoints.label: Flow metrics + honest charts
expectedPoints.description: Lead/cycle time, throughput, CFD for bottlenecks; burnup keeps scope changes visible.

expectedPoints.label: Names concrete anti-patterns
expectedPoints.description: Velocity comparison/pressure, scope creep, skipped retros, agile-in-name-only.
